head_coach.name: Todd Monken
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Adaptable, personnel-driven offensive mind. Run-game foundation with spread/Air Raid passing concepts. Adjusts personnel groupings to roster talent. Physical, run-first identity with explosive play-action and vertical passing layered on top.

offensive_coordinator.name: Travis Switzer
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Monken/Ravens run game tree; served as Ravens run game coordinator under Monken 2023-2025

defensive_coordinator.name: Mike Rutenberg
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Robert Saleh/Gus Bradley 4-3 attacking front tree; influenced by Jeff Ulbrich; worked under Saleh in Jacksonville, San Francisco, and New York Jets

offense.scheme_family: Monken hybrid: Air Raid passing concepts married to a physical, varied run game. Personnel-flexible system that adapts groupings to roster talent. Run-game-first identity with play-action vertical shots.
offense.base_personnel: 12/21 heavy with varied groupings; in Baltimore ran league-low 11 personnel (29.9%) in 2025, heavy 12 (35.9%), 21 (18.6%), and 22 (8.3%); Cleveland expected to maintain similar multi-personnel approach though may trend toward more 11 given receiver needs
offense.run_scheme: Inside zone read as primary concept, supplemented by power, counter, and gap schemes. QB-designed runs and read-option integral. Switzer as run game coordinator imports Ravens' rushing philosophy that led the NFL in rushing yards in 2 of 3 Monken seasons.
offense.pass_concepts: Play-action heavy off run-game looks, vertical shots, bunch/compressed formations, Air Raid four-verts and spacing concepts, RPO game, no-huddle tempo to stress defensive substitutions
offense.te_usage: Critical to scheme. Heavy 12 and 22 personnel usage means TE is a featured weapon. In Baltimore, Andrews/Likely were flexed into slot and split wide creating mismatches. TEs used as both inline blockers and move pieces. Expect TE to be an integral pass-catching target.
offense.rb_usage: Bell cow primary runner in a run-first system. Monken featured Derrick Henry as a workhorse in Baltimore. RBs also featured more in passing game under Monken than predecessor, with increased check-down and intermediate route work. Quinshon Judkins expected to be featured back.

defense.alignment: Even front (4-down), wide-9 alignment with DEs lined up outside offensive tackles to maximize edge rush speed. Continuity from Schwartz's system explicitly maintained.
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-3 DE hands-in-dirt; Myles Garrett is the prototype as a two-time DPOY. DEs aligned wide in wide-9 technique to get upfield quickly. Low blitz rate (18.4% in 2025, among league's lowest) relies on four-man pass rush.
defense.coverage_base: Man-heavy under Schwartz (Cover 1 on 40.8% of snaps in 2025, highest in NFL). Cover 3 at 23.7%. Rutenberg may introduce more Cover 4/quarters (Falcons used quarters at 6th-highest rate in 2025) and slightly more pre-snap disguise, but base remains single-high man coverage behind dominant front four.
defense.blitz_tendency: light (18.4% blitz rate in 2025, 2nd-lowest in NFL; scheme trusts four-man rush with Garrett, Graham, Collins)
defense.lb_role: Off-ball LBs are coverage/run-stopping, NOT pass rushers. ILBs (Carson Schwesinger, Devin Bush) asked to make calls, cover in man, play edges, and blitz sparingly. OLBs do not exist in this 4-3; pass rush comes entirely from DL. Rutenberg developed LBs CJ Mosley and Quincy Williams with Jets into elite coverage/tackling LBs.
defense.safety_usage: Grant Delpit and Ronnie Hickman as starting safeties. Schwartz deployed three safeties in nickel looks. Strong safety operates near box. Delpit led team with 108 tackles in 2024; Hickman had 100 tackles in 2025. Safeties are physical, tackle-first players in this scheme but not primary blitzers given low blitz rate.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Monken's offense is built around heavy 12 and 22 personnel where TEs are featured pass-catchers, not just blockers. In Baltimore, Mark Andrews had a first-team All-Pro season (1,361 receiving yards in 2021 under Switzer's TE coaching), and Isaiah Likely was used as essentially a WR in a TE body — flexed into the slot, split wide, and aligned in the backfield. Monken called Likely/Andrews 'tough matchup guys' and ran 12 personnel on 35.9% of plays in 2025. David Njoku has departed Cleveland, creating a massive TE need. Any receiving TE will be a day-one featured weapon in this offense. Elite route running, hands, and separation from the TE position will be valued near WR-level.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Cleveland runs a pure 4-3 with wide-9 alignment where ALL edge rushing comes from hands-in-dirt DEs, not stand-up OLBs. The blitz rate was the 2nd-lowest in the NFL (18.4%). Off-ball LBs like Schwesinger and Bush are asked to cover, tackle, and make calls — not rush the passer. Rutenberg developed traditional coverage LBs (Mosley, Williams) at his prior stops. An LB with pass rush traits would have very limited scheme-based opportunities to rush in this defense. There is no OLB edge role to convert into.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Monken increased RB pass game involvement significantly over the Greg Roman era in Baltimore. RBs were featured more on check-downs, intermediate routes, and even downfield concepts. However, Cleveland's identity will still be run-first (Ravens led NFL in rushing 2 of 3 years under Monken), and the primary back (Judkins) is valued first as a runner. Receiving ability is a plus trait, not the primary trait. Moderate boost for backs who can catch.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Under Schwartz, the Browns deployed three-safety nickel packages and used the strong safety near the box. Grant Delpit (108 tackles in 2024) and Ronnie Hickman (100 tackles in 2025) play physical, tackle-first safety roles. However, the low blitz rate means safeties are not frequently asked to blitz or rush. Rutenberg's background with Saleh values versatile safeties — he helped develop rookie Xavier Watts (5 INTs) in Atlanta. A safety who can cover, tackle in the box, and play slot coverage has value, but this is not a scheme that asks safeties to be LB/EDGE hybrids. Moderate boost for versatile, physical safeties.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Cleveland's scheme creates a unique draft value profile: (1) TIGHT END is a premium need and premium scheme fit — with Njoku departing and Monken's 12/22-personnel-heavy system, a receiving TE like Kenyon Sadiq would function as a WR-equivalent weapon and should be valued 10-15 picks higher for CLE than for teams running light 11-personnel offenses; (2) OFFENSIVE LINE is the single biggest roster need, with Bitonio, Teller, Pocic, and multiple tackles hitting free agency — Monken's zone-based run scheme values athletic OL who can move laterally; (3) The 4-3 wide-9 defense with its league-lowest blitz rate means pass-rushing LBs have almost NO scheme fit here — LB prospects should be valued for coverage, tackling, and instincts, not pass rush; (4) DE/EDGE remains premium — Schwartz/Rutenberg's system is literally built around dominant 4-down rushing, and any edge prospect with explosion and bend pairs perfectly opposite Myles Garrett; (5) RB receiving traits have moderate value given Monken's increased back involvement in the pass game vs. the old Cleveland offensive approach; (6) SAFETY with coverage versatility fits the Rutenberg mold (Saleh tree values rangy, ball-hawking safeties), but the low-blitz scheme limits hybrid/blitz safety value.
